steps.specialNote: نَفْس is feminine in Arabic. It appears in many forms: النَّفْسُ الْمُطْمَئِنَّةُ (the tranquil soul), لَا تَجْزِي نَفْسٌ عَن نَّفْسٍ (no soul will avail another soul).

ayahCards.arabicText: وَاتَّقُوا يَوْمًا لَّا تَجْزِي نَفْسٌ عَن نَّفْسٍ شَيْئًا
ayahCards.translation: And fear a Day when no soul will avail another soul at all.

steps.question: Both النَّفْسُ and الْمُطْمَئِنَّةُ end in ـُ. What rule is demonstrated here?
choices: An adjective must match its noun in case, gender, number, and definiteness
All words in a sentence take the same case ending
Feminine words always end in damma
Definite nouns always take the nominative
steps.explanation: In Arabic, adjectives agree with their nouns in all four features: case (marfu/mansoob/majroor), gender (masc/fem), number (sing/dual/plural), and definiteness (definite/indefinite).

steps.question: نَفْسٍ in عَن نَّفْسٍ ends in ـٍ (double kasra/tanwin). What does this indicate?
choices: Majroor (genitive) + indefinite — after the preposition عَن
steps.explanation: Tanwin (nunation — the doubled diacritics ـٌ ـً ـٍ) marks indefiniteness. ـٍ = indefinite + majroor. ـٌ = indefinite + marfu. ـً = indefinite + mansoob.
